coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of chitosan-based fluorescent hydrogel membranes cross-linked with bisbenzaldehyde for efficient selective detection and adsorption of Fe2+
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Dequan
Lv, Shenghua
Zuo, Jingjing
Liang, Shan
Wang, Jialin
He, Tingxiang
Liu, Leipeng
coredata.dc:description: Chitosan, as a natural biomass material, is green, recyclable, sustainable and well biocompatible. The molecular chain is rich in active groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups, and its preparation of fluorescent probes has the advantages of biocompatibility and efficient detection performance. In this study, a bis(benzaldehyde) (BHD) fluorescent functional molecule was designed. Then a series of fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel films (CSBHD) were prepared using chitosan as raw material and BHD as cross-linking agent. As a fluorescent probe for metal ions, CSBHD was able to efficiently detect Fe2+ with a linear correlation of fluorescence intensity in the range of 0–160 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.55 μM. Moreover, it has excellent adsorption performance for Fe2+ ions, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 223.5 g/mg at 500 mg/L Fe2+ concentration. Finally, we characterised the structure and microscopic morphology of CSBHD films and found that CSBHD as a hydrogel film has a high cross-linking density, good water resistance, excellent thermal stability, strong resistance to swelling, and excellent stability in cycling tests. Hence, it has great potential for application in adsorption and detection of Fe2+ ions. It also provides a good strategy for the application of chitosan based fluorescent probe materials in environmental monitoring and heavy metal ion adsorption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Fluorescent hydrogels
Membrane
Fe2+
Adsorption
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel film
Preparation of fluorescent chitosan hydrogel film (CSBHD)
Characterization of BHD and CSBHD hydrogel films
Mechanical properties, swellability and hydrophilicity analysis
The optical characteristics of CSBHD hydrogel films
Selectivity of CSBHD10 for Fe2+ detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Shaanxi Province Regional Innovation Capability Guidance Program
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21276152 ) and the Shaanxi Province Regional Innovation Capability Guidance Program (No. 2021QFY04-04 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process of BHD and CSBHD fluorescent chitosan hydrogel film.
(a) FT-IR spectra of BHD, (b) FT-IR spectra of CS and CSBHD, (c) XRD patterns of CS0 and CSBHD, (d) 1H NMR spectra of BHD, (e) 13C NMR spectra of BHD.
XPS analysis of CSBHD10 hydrogel film.
SEM images of the CSBHD10 chitosan film surface: (a, b, c, d) planar surface, (a’, b’, c’, d’) fracture site.

CSBHD hydrogel films: (a) Water vapor permeability, (b) MES of hydrogel films, (c) contact angle, and (d) stress-strain, (e1-e3) Photos of CSBHD hydrogel film bending, spreading and under UV light, (f1-f3) Changes of CS0 hydrogel membranes immersed in acetate buffer (pH = 3.2) for 0.5 h, 3 h, and 6 h, (g1-g3) Changes of CSBHD10 hydrogel membranes immersed in acetate buffer (pH = 3.2) for 0.5 h, 3 h, and 24 h.

(a) Fluorescence performance of different CSBHD fluorescent hydrogel films, (b) Physical fluorescence map of the CSBHD10 fluorescent hydrogel film, (c) Coordination Mechanism of CSBHD10 with Fe2+, (d) FT-IR spectra of CSBHD10 + Fe2+.

Detection performance of CSBHD10 fluorescent hydrogel for heavy metal ions. (a) Histogram of the fluorescence response of the hydrogel to different metals, (b) Effect of different concentrations of Fe2+ metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the hydrogel film, (c) Linear fit to different concentrations of Fe2+ metal ions, (d) Quenching of different metal ions on the fluorescence film.
Effect of fluorescence response at various (a) pH, (b) time, and (c) mixed metals, (d) Laser confocal image of CSBHD10.

(a) Adsorption capacity of the CSBHD10 fluorescent hydrogel film for different metal ions, (b) Adsorption capacity of the CSBHD10 fluorescent hydrogel film for Fe2+ (500 mg/L) under different times (insert: pseudo-second-order model fit), (c) Adsorption capacity of the CSBHD10 fluorescent hydrogel film for Fe2+ with different initial concentrations (insert: Freundlich model fit), (d) Cyclic adsorption capacity of the CSBHD10 fluorescent hydrogel film for Fe2+ ions.
Schematic illustration of the recognition mechanism of Fe2+ detection by CSBHD fluorescent hydrogel film.
Schematic illustration of the (a) preparation process and (b) detection mechanism of the CSBHD hydrogel membrane probe.
Reagents and contents of different hydrogel films.
Comparison of Fe3+/Fe2+ detection and adsorption properties with other chitosan-based biomaterials.
Kinetic parameters of pseudo-first and pseudo-second order equations for Fe2+ adsorption by CSBHD10.
Parameters related to Langmuir and Freundlich models of Fe2+ adsorption by CSBHD10.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of chitosan-based fluorescent hydrogel membranes cross-linked with bisbenzaldehyde for efficient selective detection and adsorption of Fe2+

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'an 710021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering
Shaanxi University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science and Technology, Xi'’an 710021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chitosan, as a natural biomass material, is green, recyclable, sustainable and well biocompatible. The molecular chain is rich in active groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups, and its preparation of fluorescent probes has the advantages of biocompatibility and efficient detection performance. In this study, a bis(benzaldehyde) (BHD) fluorescent functional molecule was designed. Then a series of fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel films (CSBHD) were prepared using chitosan as raw material and BHD as cross-linking agent. As a fluorescent probe for metal ions, CSBHD was able to efficiently detect Fe2+ with a linear correlation of fluorescence intensity in the range of 0–160 μM, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.55 μM. Moreover, it has excellent adsorption performance for Fe2+ ions, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 223.5 g/mg at 500 mg/L Fe2+ concentration. Finally, we characterised the structure and microscopic morphology of CSBHD films and found that CSBHD as a hydrogel film has a high cross-linking density, good water resistance, excellent thermal stability, strong resistance to swelling, and excellent stability in cycling tests. Hence, it has great potential for application in adsorption and detection of Fe2+ ions. It also provides a good strategy for the application of chitosan based fluorescent probe materials in environmental monitoring and heavy metal ion adsorption.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron, as the most abundant transition metal element in living organisms, plays a vital role in maintaining the normal functioning of cells in the body. Iron in living organisms mainly exists in the form of ferrous ions (Fe2+). And the imbalance of Fe2+ content may lead to a variety of pathologies such as anaemia, cancer, and cardiovascular diseases. Therefore, the development of real-time and rapid detection of Fe2+ is essential. Traditional metal ion detection methods include electrochemical techniques [1,2] and atomic absorption spectrometry [3]. Although these detection techniques have gained more applications, these have the disadvantages of expensive equipment and cumbersome operation. In contrast, fluorescent probe materials are the most promising method due to their high sensitivity, simplicity and rapidity [4–6]. They play an important role in environmental detection, medical diagnostics and biological imaging [7–10]. However, traditional organic molecule fluorescent probes often suffer from certain defects or deficiencies such as toxicity and poor chemical stability, which limits their applications [11,12]. Therefore, biomass-based fluorescent probes have gradually become the focus of research in recent years. Fluorescent probe materials prepared using cellulose, chitosan and lignin as precursors not only have good physicochemical properties, such as biocompatibility, hydrophilicity, low cost and biodegradability, but also improve the range of fluorescent probe applications [13,14].

Chitosan (CS), a copolymer consisting of glucosamine and N-acetylglucosamine units randomly distributed on a backbone, is a natural polymer that is abundant, biodegradable and renewable. The long chains of CS is rich in reactive groups such as amino and hydroxyl groups, which have stable physicochemical properties and biocompatibility [15,16]. Hence, it has a very wide range of applications in the fields of biomedicine, food processing, water treatment and environmental detection [17–19]. Especially in recent years, chitosan-based fluorescent probe materials have been developed significantly [20–22]. Due to the good reactivity of -NH2 in the chitosan structure, it can generate fluorescence by cross-linking the polymer chains with NH2 and introducing fluorescent groups [23]. For example, Belali et al. [24]. et al. used 3,5-Diformyl-4,4-difluoro-4-bora-3a,4a-diaza-s-indacene as a functional cross-linker to prepare novel chitosan-based hydrogels with fluorescent functionality. The photochemical properties of the hydrogels were improved by cross-linking chitosan via Schiff base reaction. Therefore, the preparation of fluorescent probes from chitosan has the advantages of low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and superior detection performance [25,26]. Among them, chitosan hydrogel fluorescent materials with simple synthesis process, efficient detection performance and easy to use, so they are widely used in sensor [27] and catalysis [28].

Recently, the researchers have developed a series of chitosan hydrogel fluorescent probe materials, which have been widely used for the detection of heavy metal ions and have demonstrated excellent recognition and detection performance [29,30]. Fan et al. [31] prepared chitosan fluorescent hydrogels by combining tripyridine-containing aldehydes with chitosan via an acid condensation reaction. This hydrogel fluorescent sensor can be used to monitor Fe2+ in organisms and the environment with a detection limit of 0.28 ppm, and this approach provides a new avenue for the development of sensitive hydrogel chemosensors. Yang et al. [32] prepared a fluorescent probe based on Schiff base, which can be effectively used to recognise Cu2+, and has a detection limit of 1.54 × 10−8 M. The development of these probe materials provides a good research basis for future chitosan-based fluorescent probe studies. Based on these existing researches, it can be seen that chitosan-based fluorescent probes show good performance in the detection of heavy metal ions. In particular, the fluorescent probes based on Schiff bases are better able to specifically complex with heavy metal ions for identification and detection purposes. Due to the unique amino structure in the chitosan structure that can form imine bonds with aldehydes, it provides a basis for the development of novel chitosan-based Schiff base fluorescent probes with high detection efficiency and versatility.

Here, inspired by chitosan fluorescent probe materials, we developed novel bis(benzaldehyde) (BHD) fluorescent molecules by a simple preparation method. Then a series of chitosan hydrogel films (CSBHD) with excellent fluorescent properties were prepared using BHD as a cross-linking agent (Scheme 1 ). Subsequently, the effects of different levels of cross-linker BHD on the structure and fluorescence properties of CSBHD films were investigated. The selectivity, immunity and detection limit of CSBHD for Fe2+, and the adsorption characteristics of CSBHD on heavy metal ions, especially on Fe2+ ions were also investigated. It makes full use of the amine group structure in the chitosan structure and cross-links it with dialdehyde to form a novel Schiff base biomass fluorescent probe material, which provides a new research strategy for the detection and adsorption of heavy metal ions.

Chitosan (molecular weight: 91000, degree of deacetylation: 90.4 %), 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde, hexamethylene isocyanate (HDI), Glacial acetic acid, Magnesium sulfate, Sodium hydroxide purchased from Qiangke China Chemical; Copper chloride, Ferric chloride, Nickel chloride, Calcium Chloride, Mercury sulfate, Lead chloride, Aluminum chloride, (analytical grade, Tianjin Comeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.); Cobalt Chloride, Potassium Chloride, Zinc Chloride, Silver Nitrate Magnesium Chloride, Chromium Chloride, Manganese Chloride (analytical grade, Xilong Science and Technology); Tetrahydrofuran (THF), Acetonitrile, purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent chitosan-based hydrogel film

ce:section.ce:para: 2.44 g (20 mmol) of 4-hydroxybenzaldehyde was added to a 100 mL round-bottomed flask and 60 mL of acetonitrile was added and stirred to dissolve. 1.68 g (10 mmol) of hexamethylene diisocyanate (HDI) was added dropwise within 30 min and the reaction was continued at 60 °C for 8 h. After the reaction was completed, it was filtered under reduced pressure with the vacuum pump, and the white solid obtained was washed three times with 10 mL acetonitrile to remove impurities, and vacuum dried for 12 h to obtain white solid powder bis(benzaldehyde) (BHD), which was sealed and kept for spare use. BHD: 1H NMR (600 MHz, DMSO‑d) δ 11.01 (s, 2H), 8.97 (d, J = 8.6 Hz, 4H), 8.45–8.25 (m, 4H), 4.42 (m, 2H), 4.20–4.05 (m, 4H), 3.55 (q, J = 1.9 Hz, 1H), 2.61–2.47 (m, 4H), 2.41–2.31 (m, 4H). 13C NMR (151 MHz, DMSO‑d) δ 189.91, 153.91, 151.42, 130.91, 128.96, 120.15, 38.42, 27.03, 23.88.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent chitosan hydrogel film (CSBHD)

ce:section.ce:para: The structural characterization of the chitosan, crosslinker BHD and CSBHD hydrogel films was first carried out by total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (VECTOR model 70, Bruker, Germany) over a scanning range of 3500 cm−1-1000 cm−1, scan number of 50 times and resolution of 1 cm-l.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The crystal structures of different hydrogels were observed using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker, Germany). The hydrogel film samples were cut into 3 cm × 3 cm square samples. Measurements were performed on a copper target with a tube voltage of 40 kV, a tube current of 30 mA, a scanning 2θ angle of 5–40°, a step size of 0.02, a scanning speed of 4°/min, and a temperature of 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:para: The CSBHD hydrogel film samples were cut into circular samples with a radius of 0.5 cm at a temperature of 25 °C, then dried and the experimental samples were observed under vacuum conditions using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, model S-4800, HITACHI, Japan).

The surface chemical composition of the sample was studied by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Source gun type: Al K Alpha; Spot size: 400 μm; Analyser mode CAE: pass energy 150.0 eV; Energy step size 1.000 eV; Number of energy steps: 1361.

According to the standard ASTM E96–05, a certain amount of distilled water was added to a 100 mL conical flask, and the hydrogel film sample was wrapped around the mouth of the conical flask and tied tightly with a rubber band, and its mass was weighed. The conical flasks were placed in a desiccator with discoloured silica gel at the bottom and the desiccator was placed in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. The mass was weighed and the water vapor transmission rate was calculated by Eq. (1):
(1)
T =
∆ m ∙ b
S ∙ t
where T - water vapor transmission coefficient (g/(mm ∙ d)); Δm- Weight reduction of conical flasks (g); b- Sample thickness (mm); S- Area of film wrapped around the test tube (mm2); t- Placement time (d).

Tensile strength according to the standard GB/T528–1998, the film samples were cut into long strips of 0.5 cm × 3 cm. At 25 °C, use the universal tensile testing machine at a speed of 25 mm/min to carry out tensile tests on the specimen, the test takes the average of five groups of specimens.

After drying the 2 cm × 2 cm hydrogel film to a constant mass m0, the film samples were placed in deionized water, and the volume of deionized water was kept at a constant thermal temperature for each test group. The samples were removed after 24 h of water absorption and swelling, and the water was wiped off the surface and then weighed [33]. The swelling degree of the sample was calculated according to Eq. (2):
(2)
S d
=
m 1
−
m 0
ρ
m 0
× 100 %
where: S
d - swelling of the sample; m
0 - the mass of the sample without treatment; m
1 - The mass of the sample after water absorption and swelling; ρ − Solvent density (g/mL).

Fluorescence intensity, selectivity and detection limits of hydrogel membrane samples were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer Edinburgh FS5. Firstly, to investigate the fluorescence selectivity of CS-BHD hydrogel membrane to Fe2+, a series of metal ion (Hg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Co2+, K+) solutions with a concentration of 0.01 mol/L were prepared. The CSBHD hydrogel membrane was then cut into rectangular block samples. The samples were placed into several identical test tubes with 4.0 mL of H2O, and then 100 μL of different metal ion solutions were added to each test tube. The fluorescence emission spectra of the mixed sample system with different metal ions were then measured at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm using a fluorescence spectrometer and the fluorescence intensity was recorded. And UV–visible spectrophotometer Lambda 25 was used to obtain the UV spectra of the hydrogel film. In order to test the concentration effect of CSBHD with Fe2+, the gel membrane samples were added into different concentrations of Fe2+ solutions (0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300, 400 μmol/L), and then the change of fluorescence intensity was detected, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by linear fitting. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as follows:
LOD = 3 δ / K
where δ is the standard deviation of the blank standard and K is the slope of the calibration curve.

Due to the unique adsorption capacity of CSBHD10 for heavy metal ions, the adsorption properties of the hydrogel membrane for heavy metal ions were further investigated. 10 mL of Fe2+ solution (500 mg/L) was placed in a sample vial and 20 mg of CSBHD membrane was added for adsorption experiments, followed by adsorption experiments at different times (10, 20, 40, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, and 200 min) to determine the adsorption kinetics of Fe2+ in sequence. At the end of the experiment the solution was filtered and collected and finally the concentration of metal ions in the filtrate was measured using ICP-OES. Determination of Fe2+ adsorption isotherms: 10 mL of Fe2+ solution at various initial concentrations (50–1000 mg/L) were placed in a sample vial and 20 mg of CSBHD10 membrane was added. After 12 h of adsorption at constant temperature, the solution was filtered and measured by ICP-OES. In the recovery test experiments, the hydrogel membrane was separated by filtration and then immersed in EDTA solution for regeneration for five adsorption-desorption cycles.
Adsorption kinetics:

In order to investigate the adsorption mechanism of Fe2+ in hydrogel membranes, we carried out the proposed first-order model and the proposed second-order model experiments, respectively, and the pseudo-first-order kinetic model was formulated as follows:
ln
Q e
−
Q t
= ln
Q e
−
k 1
t

The equations of the pseudo-second-order dynamical model:
t
Q t
=
1
K 2
Q e 2
+
t
Q e
where Q
e and Q
t are the adsorption capacity (mg/g) of the hydrogel membrane at equilibrium time t and time t, respectively. K
1 and K
2 of the rate constants for the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics, respectively.
Adsorption isotherm:

In addition, we have used the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models to describe isothermal adsorption equilibrium data. Langmuir isotherm mainly describes the monolayer adsorption profile on the surface of a homogeneous adsorbent, and the Langmuir isotherm equation is expressed as follows:
Q e
=
Q m
K L
C e
1 +
K L
C e
where Q
m (mg/g) is the maximum adsorption capacity and K
L is Langmuir's constant.C
0 and C
e (mg/L) are the concentrations of Fe2+ in the initial and equilibrium solutions, respectively.

The Freundlich model is an empirical formulation that assumes the presence of non-uniform physical adsorption on the surface of the adsorbent, and the specific equations of the Freundlich model are as follows:
Q e
=
K F
C e
1 n
where K
F is the Freundlich constant representing the amount adsorbed on the adsorbent and n is the dimensionless exponent of the Freundlich equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of BHD and CSBHD hydrogel films
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared bis(benzaldehyde) (BHD) and CSBHD hydrogel films were characterised by the FT-IR spectra and results are shown in Fig. 2 . The Fig. 2a shows the FT-IR spectrum of the cross-linker BHD, as seen from the results, there was the -NH stretching vibration peak present at 3321 cm−1 with a large peak intensity, which belongs to the stretching vibration of BHD reacting with p-hydroxybenzaldehyde to form amide bonds. There was an obvious absorption peak at 1702 cm−1, which is characteristic of the -CHO stretching vibration in the BHD structure. The corresponding moderate absorption peak at 1215 cm−1 was characteristic of the CN stretching vibration in the amide ester bond. This indicates that the p-hydroxybenzaldehyde and the isocyanate HDI successfully carried out the amido-esterification reaction to form BHD. Fig. 2b shows the infrared spectra of hydrogel membranes compared to chitosan membranes (CS0) with new peaks at 1640 cm−1 for CSBHD5, CSBHD10 and CSBHD20, which belong to CN stretching vibrations [34,35]. It can also be seen that with the increase of BHD content, the intensity at the peak increased. However, the absorption peak of aldehyde group around 1700 cm−1 was also present in CSBHD20, which may be due to the presence of a small amount of unreacted dialdehyde group in the CSBHD20 hydrogel film due to the higher BHD content. The 1217 cm−1 peak belongs to the CO stretching vibration peak of the amide-ester bond in the cross-linking agent BHD, which was enhanced with the addition of 5 %, 10 % and 20 % of the cross-linking agent BHD, which can be a preliminary indication that the synthesis of the CSBHD hydrogel film was successful. To further investigate the chemical structure of the fluorescent hydrogel films, we characterised them with XRD, and the results are shown in Fig. 2c. The pure chitosan film has two broad peaks near 2θ = 12 and 21 (characteristic of the II crystalline phase), showing crystallinity with the chitosan structure. Compared to pure chitosan membranes, the broad peaks at 2θ = 12 and 21 are significantly attenuated in dialdehyde crosslinked hydrogels. This suggests that the crystallinity of the original chitosan structure is disrupted by crosslinking through BHD. In addition, CSBHD showed new sharp reflection bands around 2θ = 8 and 12, which may be related to the increase in the distance between the chitosan molecular chains by the introduction of the dialdehyde BHD. This further suggests that the cross-linking effect of BHD on chitosan leads to the disruption of the structure of the chitosan molecular chains and the decrease in crystallinity. Similarly, when the content of the cross-linker BHD reaches 20 %, the diffraction peaks of a small amount of dialdehyde BHD appeared in CSBHD20, which is consistent with the FT-IR results, which may be due to the fact that the higher content of BHD may not react completely with some BHD.

To further investigate the surface chemical structure composition of CSBHD hydrogel films, we characterised CSBHD10 by XPS analysis. As shown in Fig. 3 , the C1s spectra of XPS spectra (Fig. 3b) at 284.5 eV, 286.2 eV and 287.5 eV correspond to C-C/OH, C-N/C-O-C and CN functional groups, respectively. In the C1s spectrum (Fig. 3c), 531.1 eV and 532.4 eV were CO and CO groups, respectively, which were mainly derived from the abundant OH, C-O-C and CO groups in the CSBHD. Moreover, in the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3d), 399.2 eV and 401.1 eV belonged to CN and C-N=C bonds, respectively, which indicated that the BHD cross-linker was successfully introduced into chitosan. Combined with the FT-IR spectra and XPS spectral data, we were able to further confirm the successful synthesis of CSBHD hydrogel films.

The morphology and structure of hydrogel films were characterised by SEM, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(a, a’) shows the scanning electron microscope images of the front and side of the pure chitosan film CS0, which is flatter and smoother on the front side and denser on the inside. Compared with CS0, it can be seen that Fig. 4(b-d) shows the scanning electron microscope images of the surface of CSBHD10 at different magnifications. From the results, it can be seen that the front surface of CSBHD10 hydrogel films under different magnifications was overall flat and smooth, which indicated that CSBHD10 had good film-forming properties [36]. Fig. 4(b’-d’) shows the morphology of the CSBHD10 hydrogel film from the side, and the CSBHD10 has an obvious internal 3D cross-linking structure and abundant pore structure. It suggests that the dialdehyde BHD has a significant cross-linking effect, and the three-dimensional cross-linking structure appropriately increases the water vapor transmission rate while decreasing the MES value. In addition, this uniform and dense cross three-dimensional network structure has increased the internal specific surface area of the film, which facilitates the attachment of heavy metal ions to the film. This lays the foundation for later adsorption and detection of metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties, swellability and hydrophilicity analysis
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the physicochemical properties of CSBHD hydrogel films. Firstly, we tested the water vapor transmission rate (WVP) of the films, and the results (Fig. 5a) showed that the WVP of the CSBHD hydrogel films with the addition of the BHD cross-linking agent was significantly reduced compared to the pure chitosan films. The WVP value of the CSBHD film decreased and then slightly increased with the increase of cross-linker content, but the overall range of variation was small. The results show that CSBHD15 films with 15 % BHD cross-linker have higher density and better barrier properties to oxygen and water. In addition, the swelling characteristics of the CSBHD hydrogel films were tested and the results are shown in Fig. 5b. The swelling values in deionized water and phosphate buffer solution (PBS) at pH 7.4 were similar, with a mass-equilibrium swelling (MES) value of approximately 1. This may be due to the introduced BHD which increased the cross-link density in the CSBHD hydrogel film and therefore provided better resistance to swelling. However, in acetate buffer solution at pH 4.5, the MES value of CSBHD hydrogel membrane was about 5, whereas the CS0 membrane gradually dissolved after >12 h. This may be due to the fact that CSBHD cross-linked by BHD was more stable in acetate buffer solution than pure CS0 membrane. Nevertheless, the imine structure was more stable in alkaline environments and less stable in acidic solutions, and thus the MES values of CSBHD10 were larger in acidic environments. In addition, according to previous studies, if the integrity of the hydrogel membrane can be maintained in acidic solution, it indicates that the chitosan gel membrane has a good degree of cross-linking. Therefore, we also carried out the immersion experiments of hydrogels in acetate buffer (pH = 3.2), as shown in Fig. 5(f1-f3), the CS0 hydrogel membrane started to dissolve gradually at 3 h, and was basically dissolved at 6 h hours. On the contrary, the CSBHD hydrogel membrane still maintains the intact hydrogel membrane structure at 24 h in the acetate buffer immersion experiment (Fig. 5(g1-g3)), which indirectly indicates that the cross-linking effect of BHD on CSBHD gives it excellent chemical stability.

Besides, appropriate hydrophilicity can help to expand the application range of hydrogel films, and we have also investigated the hydrophilic wettability of CSBHD hydrogel membrane by contact angle experiments [37]. As shown in Fig. 5c, the WCA values of CSBHD hydrogel membrane decreased and then increased with the increase of cross-linking agent BHD. And the decrease of WCA values indicated that the hydrophilicity of the CSBHD film was enhanced and the wetting ability was increased. The WCA value of CS0 was θca = 89°, whereas the WCA values of CSBHD hydrogel films were 85°, 76°, and 79°at 5 %, 10 %, and 15 % BHD content, respectively, thus the BHD content of 10 % provided better wettability and hydrophilicity. This change may be attributed to the BHD introduced amide ester bonding structure in the cross-linked modified chitosan films, which increased the polarity of the CSBHD membrane. However, the cross-linking density increases with increasing BHD content and the benzene ring structure was introduced, which resulted in a hydrogel film with good water resistance. Overall, the introduction of imine units into the cross-linked modified chitosan structure of BHD resulted in good water resistance and certain acid resistance of CSBHD, which provided unique fluorescent properties and increased its range of applications [38,39]. As can be seen in Fig. 5 (e1-e3), the hydrogel membrane can be well bent and flattened, which indicates that it has a certain degree of flexibility. In addition, in Fig. 5e3, it can be seen that compared with the pure chitosan membrane CS0, the distinctive blue fluorescence of CSBHD can be clearly seen under UV light. Subsequently, we explored the mechanical properties of the CSBHD film (Fig. 5d). The tensile test results showed that the CSBHD had good mechanical properties compared with the pure chitosan film, and with the increase of the cross-linking agent BHD content, the mechanical properties firstly increased gradually, and then tended to be stable, and the maximum tensile strength was 21 MPa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical characteristics of CSBHD hydrogel films
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6a, CSBHD in preliminary fluorescence experiments can be seen that after being cross-linked by fluorescent functional BHD, the CSBHD hydrogel film has a significant blue fluorescence at 365 nm under UV light. Combined with the fluorescence spectra, it can be seen that CSBHD5 showed a fluorescence emission peak at the emission wavelength at 480 nm, which was not found in the CS0 film. In addition, and with the increase of the cross-linker BHD content, the fluorescence intensity was significantly increased and blue-shifted at the same time, and the fluorescence emission wavelength of the CSBHD20 film was 470 nm. This indicates that the CSBHD hydrogel film has good fluorescence properties after the introduced cross-linker BHD with fluorescence function. Combined with the fluorescence excitation and excitation spectra in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence excitation wavelength of CSBHD10 hydrogel was found to be Ex = 405 nm, and the emission wavelength was found to be Em = 475 nm. Accordingly, the CSBHD hydrogel films exhibited blue fluorescence under UV irradiation and had a quantum yield of QY = 2.4 %, which further indicated that the BHD crosslinked hydrogel films has good fluorescent properties. To further study the detection mechanism of CSBHD hydrogel film on Fe2+, we carried out the UV and FT-IR spectra of the CSBHD10 hydrogel film and the changes of these spectra in combination with Fe2+. As shown in Fig. 6c, the CSBHD has a broad absorption peak at 285 nm, and the UV absorption peak of CSBHD10 + Fe2+ was blue shifted after the addition of Fe2+, and the absorption peak was roughly at 277 nm. This may be due to the occurrence of mutual complexation between the CSBHD10 hydrogel membrane and Fe2+, which caused a blue shift in its UV absorption. In Fig. 6d, it can be seen that after the binding of CSBHD10 hydrogel film with Fe2+, a new characteristic peak appeared in the FT-IR spectrum at 1582.6 cm−1 and the intensity of the characteristic peak increased with the increase of Fe2+ concentration [40,41]. This further confirmed the existence of mutual complexation between CSBHD and Fe2+, which was also consistent with the later results of the chemisorption mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of CSBHD10 for Fe2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further understand the fluorescence performance of CSBHD membrane, we chose CSBHD10 to further investigate their ability to detect heavy metal ions. 2 mg of CSBHD10 film was immersed in 4 mL of aqueous solution, and then 100 μL (10−2 M) of metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Co2+, K+) were added to adjust the pH between 6.5 and 7 (Fig. 7a). The results showed that Fe2+ could significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity of the CSBHD10, while no significant change in the fluorescence intensity of the probe occurred when other metal ions were added, this indicating that the CSBHD10 fluorescent membrane has a strong selectivity for Fe2+. Hence, CSBHD fluorescent hydrogel membrane can be used as a fluorescent probe for highly selective detection of Fe2+.

To further investigate the interaction between Fe2+ and the fluorescence intensity of CSBHD10 hydrogel membrane. We have added different concentrations (0–200 μmol/L) of Fe2+ into the CSBHD fluorescent hydrogel membrane, and then explored the changes of the fluorescence properties of the hydrogel membrane after mixing the CSBHD hydrogel membrane with different concentrations of Fe2+ (Fig. 7b) [39]. The fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with the gradual increase of Fe2+ concentration, and there was a slight red shift of the fluorescence emission peak. The effect of Fe2+ on the fluorescence intensity of CSBHD10 hydrogel membrane in the concentration range of 0–160 μmol/L was analysed by combining with Stern~Volmer formula [38]. As can be seen in Fig. 7c, CSBHD10 showed a good linear relationship with Fe2+ concentration (R2 = 0.9903) and the fitted linear equation was y = 0.9616–0.0045×. In addition, the limit of detection (LOD) for Fe2+ was calculated to be 0.55 μmol/L based on DL = 3σ/k (where σ is standard deviation of the blank group and k is the slope of the fitted linear equation). This value was much lower than the standard line value for drinking water, so that this CSBHD membrane has an important potential value as a fluorescent probe material for the detection of Fe2+. Additionally, comparing the detection and adsorption performance of Fe3+/Fe2+ with other chitosan-based biomaterials, it can be seen that the CSBHD hydrogel membrane of this report has efficient detection performance and excellent adsorption performance for Fe2+ (Table 2 ).

In order to further investigate the fluorescence stability of CSBHD10 hydrogel films, the fluorescence response effects at different pH values and different times were investigated. The CSBHD10 hydrogel films were immersed in solutions with pH values ranging from 2 to 12, and the fluorescence emission intensity of the CSBHD10 was detected at the maximum excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 8a, fluorescence intensity of CSBHD10 was strong and stable at pH 6.0–12.0, while the fluorescence is significantly weakened in acidic medium with pH < 4. This indicates that the hydrolytic stability of the hydrogel film is good in different pH media, and only pH < 4 has some effect on the fluorescence performance of the hydrogel film. This may be attributed to the fact that the imine bond was easily hydrolysed and broken in the acidic environment, which led to the unstable fluorescence performance of CSBHD10 hydrogel membrane. As can be seen from Fig. 8b, the fluorescence intensity of CSBHD10 decreased rapidly in a short time after adding Fe2+ solution to CSBHD10, and then remained stable. There was no obvious change with the extension of time, which proves that the prepared CSBHD10 can quickly identify and detect Fe2+ and has good optical stability.

In order to further investigate the capability of CSBHD hydrogel membrane to selectively recognise and detect Fe2+ in real environment. The effect of Fe2+ on the fluorescence intensity of CSBHD10 under the co-presence of different metal ions was detected. Other kinds of metal ions were added to both sets of CSBHD, and Fe2+ was added to one set. The results are shown in Fig. 8c, where the fluorescence intensity of CSBHD10 was significantly reduced because of the introduced Fe2+ compared to the addition of other different metal ions only. According to this analysis, Fe2+ was able to significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity of CSBHD under the conditions of competition of different metal ions. This suggests that when CSBHD has been used as a probe for the detection of Fe2+, the coexistence of other metal ions has little effect on the fluorescence properties, and it has a strong anti-interference ability. According to this result, CSBHD can be used for the selective determination of Fe2+ mixed with other metal ions in complex aqueous environments. In addition, fluorescence images of the CSBHD10 were obtained under a laser confocal fluorescence microscope, and the results are shown in Fig. 8d. The surface of CSBHD10 emits strong blue fluorescence with excellent fluorescence distribution, which has provided the basis for efficient detection and adsorption of Fe2+.

As shown in Fig. 9a, the adsorption of different metal ions on the CSBHD hydrogel membrane showed that the hydrogel membrane had good adsorption capacity for heavy metal ions such as Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Fe2+, in particular, the adsorption of Fe2+ was the most significant. Then, the adsorption characteristics of CSBHD10 on Fe2+ were investigated. Firstly, the effect of adsorption time on adsorption capacity was investigated as shown in Fig. 9c. Within 20 min, the adsorption of Fe2+ (500 mg/L) increased rapidly, and then the adsorption rate gradually slowed down, and finally reached the adsorption equilibrium, the maximum equilibrium adsorption amount was 223.5 g/mg, and the adsorption rate was about 89.4 %. In addition, pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order equations were used to describe the experimental data, where the correlation coefficient of the pseudo-second-order equation was R2 = 0.9996. Table 3 lists the relevant parameters for the adsorption kinetics of Fe2+, which, in combination with the relevant data from the second-order rate equations, suggests that the adsorption process of Fe2+ was mainly controlled by chemisorption processes. The adsorption isotherm of Fe2+ on CSBHD10 is shown in Fig. 9d, and the equilibrium capacity of Fe2+ adsorption gradually increases with the increase of the initial concentration of heavy metal ions [4,33,48]. We also fitted the equilibrium data of CSBHD10 adsorbed Fe2+ using Langmuir and Freundlich adsorption isotherm models, and the relevant parameters are shown in Table 4 . From the results, it can be seen that the R
2 of Freundlich equation was higher than the correlation coefficients of Langmuir equation, so the Freundlich isotherm model was more suitable for the CSBHD adsorption of Fe2+ ions [49]. In addition, n ˃ 1 in the Freundlich equation is consistent with the fact that the values of the constant n were all in the range of 1 < n < 10, which indicated that there was a physical adsorption process with non-uniform adsorption sites [50]. The synthesis suggests that there may have been two different adsorption systems for the adsorption of Fe2+ ions by CSBHD (1) adsorption through interactions such as hydrogen bonding of hydroxyl groups [51], forming a multilayered adsorption and (2) adsorption of Schiff bases in complex with Fe2+. Finally, we investigated the recycling of Fe2+ adsorption on CSBHD hydrogel, and the results were shown in Fig. 9(b), which showed that the adsorption of Fe2+ on the CSBHD hydrogel could still reach 86 % after 5 times of recycling. This indicates that the CSBHD hydrogel could be recycled many times.

The chemical structure and optical properties of the CSBHD hydrogel film were characterised in combination with the study of the chemical structure and optical properties, as well as the changes in the chemical properties after the introduction of Fe2+ were speculated. The ability of CSBHD to selectively recognise Fe2+ may be mainly due to the amide ester bond of the cross-linker BHD and the Schiff base CN formed by BHD binding with chitosan. And in aqueous solution, Fe2+ interacts with CO, CN and OH to produce the ability of coordination complexation. The corresponding mechanism simulation diagram is shown in Fig. 10 due to the different radii of different metal ions and the ability to coordinate with them, the metal ions have different ability to interact with the CSBHD fluorescent hydrogel film [52–54]. The fluorescent membrane cross-linked network structure has excellent trapping capacity for Fe2+ and can therefore be used for recognition detection and adsorption of Fe2+ ions.

In this work, a novel fluorescent chitosan hydrogel film (CSBHD) was prepared, which possesses excellent fluorescence properties and can selectively detect Fe2+ in aqueous systems, as well as adsorb and remove a variety of heavy metal ions. In addition, dissolution and mechanical experiments showed that CSBHD has good hydrophilicity and mechanical properties, thus proving that CSBHD can be recycled to avoid secondary pollution to the environment. Finally, we investigated the detection mechanism of CSBHD probes. Furthermore, we also explored the effects of different levels of cross-linkers on the performance of CSBHD fluorescent film, and the results showed that good mechanical and solubility properties could be obtained at the optimal cross-linker level. This fluorescent chitosan hydrogel membrane probe with good biocompatibility has great potential for future applications in environmental detection and biological medicine. Hence, this study and the novel chitosan membrane fluorescent probe for Fe2+ ion recognition detection and adsorption are of high research value and have important research implications in the field of environmental and food testing.

Dequan Wei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Shenghua Lv: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Resources, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Jingjing Zuo: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Shan Liang: Investigation, Data curation. Jialin Wang: Investigation. Tingxiang He: Investigation, Data curation. Leipeng Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21276152) and the Shaanxi Province Regional Innovation Capability Guidance Program (No. 2021QFY04-04).
